title: Roasts, Jokes, and Awkward Moments: The White House Dinner That Everyone Talks About
summary:
Imagine sitting at a fancy dinner with the president of the United States, and a comedian starts making jokes about him—right in front of him! That's exactly what happens every year at the White House Correspondents' Dinner.

This year is extra special because Donald Trump will attend for the first time as president. But the dinner has a long history of unforgettable moments.

In 1983, President Ronald Reagan was supposed to tell jokes. But earlier that day, he had met the bodies of Americans killed in a bombing in Lebanon. So he told the crowd, "If you'll forgive us, I'll keep my script, and I hope you'll give us a rain check." He didn't joke that night.

In 2004, President George W. Bush made fun of himself. He showed pictures of himself looking under White House furniture for weapons of mass destruction in Iraq. (Spoiler: they didn't find any.) Some people laughed, but others, like Senator John Kerry, were not amused.

Then in 2006, comedian Stephen Colbert went after President Bush hard. He said, "The greatest thing about this man is he's steady. You know where he stands. He believes the same thing Wednesday that he believed on Monday, no matter what happened Tuesday." Ouch! The room got really quiet.

So the White House Correspondents' Dinner isn't just about fancy clothes and good food. It's where the most powerful person in the world gets roasted—and sometimes roasts himself!
why_it_matters: This story shows you that even the most powerful people can be teased, and it teaches you to handle criticism with a sense of humor — or know when to be serious.

keywords.explanation: A fancy dinner where the U.S. president, journalists, and celebrities gather for jokes and roasts.
The president who attended the dinner for the first time as president this year.
A U.S. president who skipped the jokes in 1983 after meeting the bodies of Americans killed in a bombing.
The year President Reagan did not joke at the dinner because of a tragic event earlier that day.
A U.S. president who made fun of himself in 2004 by showing pictures of himself looking for weapons of mass destruction.
A comedian who delivered a harsh roast of President Bush at the 2006 dinner.

questions.question: What happens every year at the White House Correspondents' Dinner?
options: A comedian makes jokes about the president
The president gives a serious speech
questions.correct_answer: A comedian makes jokes about the president

questions.question: Why did President Ronald Reagan not tell jokes at the 1983 dinner?
options: He had met the bodies of Americans killed in a bombing
He was angry at the journalists
questions.correct_answer: He had met the bodies of Americans killed in a bombing

questions.question: What did President George W. Bush do in his 2004 jokes at the dinner?
options: He showed pictures of himself looking under furniture for weapons
He gave a serious speech about war
questions.correct_answer: He showed pictures of himself looking under furniture for weapons

questions.question: How did the room react when Stephen Colbert roasted President Bush in 2006?
Which year did the article say was special because Donald Trump attended as president?
Who was NOT amused by President Bush's 2004 jokes?

background_read: The White House Correspondents' Dinner has been held since 1921. It started as a simple gathering for journalists who covered the president.
Sometimes comedians like Stephen Colbert and Conan O'Brien have been the main entertainers. Other times, presidents have told jokes about themselves.
Not everyone thinks the dinner is a good idea. Some people say it makes journalists too friendly with politicians.
Article_Structure: WHO: The president of the United States (this year Donald Trump) and a comedian (in the past, Stephen Colbert).
WHAT: A fancy dinner where the president gets roasted by a comedian or roasts himself.
WHERE: At the White House in Washington, D.C.
WHY: The dinner is a tradition that mixes fun and politics, and it can show how people feel about the president.

perspectives.description: The dinner is a fun tradition that lets everyone laugh together, even the president. It shows that in a democracy, no one is above a good joke.
The dinner can be awkward and mean. Some comedians go too far, and the room gets uncomfortable. It might not be the best place for roasting the president.
Over the years, the dinner has changed. Some years were funny, and others were serious. It tells a story about how Americans feel about their leaders at different times.
